mnemonics.title: Data Type Sizes
mnemonics.content: ICFD - Int(4), Char(1), Float(4), Double(8) - Remember: 'I Can Find Data' or think of C=1 (C for Cookie)ar, F=4 (FOUR), D=8 (Double)

mnemonics.title: Signed vs Unsigned
mnemonics.content: SIGNED = Has SIGN (negative), UNSIGNED = No negative, double range positive. Remember: S-UN has 'S' like 'Small range' because includes negatives, UN is 'UNlimited' for positives.

mnemonics.title: Basic Types Purpose
mnemonics.content: ICFD = Integer, Character, Float, Double - Think: 'I Can Find Data' or 'In C For Data' - these are the FOUNDATION for all C programming.

acronyms.usage: Storing single characters like 'A', ASCII values, or small integers 0-255
Counting, indexing, arithmetic operations on whole numbers
Scientific calculations, sensor readings, any decimal value
High-precision scientific calculations where float is insufficient

acronyms.expansion: Type Definition - creates custom type names
acronyms.usage: Creating meaningful names like uint8_t for unsigned 8-bit integers

visualTips.visualization: Draw boxes representing bytes. Show char as 1 box, int as 4 boxes side by side. For signed/unsigned, show the MSB (Most Significant Bit) as sign bit (0=positive, 1=negative for signed) or value bit (for unsigned).
Pyramid: char (bottom, 1B) → short (2B) → int/long (4B) → long long (8B). Float sits at 4B, double at 8B. This shows relative sizes.
For n-bit signed: Range = -(2^(n-1)) to (2^(n-1)-1). For unsigned: 0 to (2^n-1). Visualize a number line showing negative and positive sides.

analogies.analogy: Think of data types as different-sized containers: char is a small cup (1L), int is a bucket (4L), double is a large tank (8L). Use the right container for your data to save space.
Signed is like a balance scale (can tip negative or positive). Unsigned is like a counter that only goes up from zero - you get double the counting capacity but no negatives.

#define is like writing a phone number on a sticky note and putting it everywhere - the number is copied everywhere. const is like having one official document that everyone references - there's an actual storage location.
